subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: This unit helps young children discover one big, wonderful idea: some things around us are alive, and some things are not alive. A goat is alive. A cooking pot is not alive. A mango tree is alive. A stone is not alive. In this guide you will lead your child, step by step, to look closely at the world Allah made and to notice what makes something a living thing.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 1 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Connect it to your child's own body. They already know they feel hungry, thirsty, and cold. Use these everyday feelings as the doorway into science. When your child says, "I am hungry," you can say, "Yes! You are a living thing, and living things need food."

concepts.title: This unit helps young children discover one big, wonderful…
concepts.explanation: This unit helps young children discover one big, wonderful idea: some things around us are alive, and some things are not alive. A goat is alive. A cooking pot is not alive. A mango tree is alive. A stone is not alive. In this guide you will lead your child, step by step, to look closely at the world Allah made and to notice what makes something a living thing.
concepts.example: Your child is 5 or 6 years old and is only just learning to read. So you are the reader, the guide, and the play-partner. You will talk, point, sing, and wonder together. Keep every lesson short, warm, and full of…

concepts.title: Your child is 5 or 6 years old and is only just learning to…
concepts.explanation: Your child is 5 or 6 years old and is only just learning to read. So you are the reader, the guide, and the play-partner. You will talk, point, sing, and wonder together. Keep every lesson short, warm, and full of looking and touching. There is no need to hurry. When your child smiles and asks a question, learning is happening.
concepts.example: This unit works well over about four weeks, doing a little each day. Read the words in bold slowly, and let your child say them back to you.

concepts.title: This unit works well over about four weeks, doing a little…
concepts.explanation: This unit works well over about four weeks, doing a little each day. Read the words in bold slowly, and let your child say them back to you.
concepts.example: You do not need to be a scientist to teach this unit. You only need to be curious with your child. When you do not know an answer, that is wonderful - you can wonder together, and you can ask your AI tutor. The most…

concepts.title: You do not need to be a scientist to teach this unit. You…
concepts.explanation: You do not need to be a scientist to teach this unit. You only need to be curious with your child. When you do not know an answer, that is wonderful - you can wonder together, and you can ask your AI tutor. The most important thing you can give a young child is your warm attention and plenty of time to look, touch, and talk. Praise their thinking, not just their answers.
concepts.example: Try to teach in short bursts of about ten to fifteen minutes. A small child cannot sit still for long, and that is normal and healthy. Do a little, then play, then come back to it later. Learning through play and real…

concepts.title: Try to teach in short bursts of about ten to fifteen…
concepts.explanation: Try to teach in short bursts of about ten to fifteen minutes. A small child cannot sit still for long, and that is normal and healthy. Do a little, then play, then come back to it later. Learning through play and real objects at home is exactly how children of this age learn best.
concepts.example: That animals (like goats, chickens, and cats) are alive, and plants (like mango and banana plants) are alive too.

terms: Something that is alive. It grows, eats, drinks, and breathes.
Something that is not alive and never was alive.
A living thing that can move about and eats to live.
A living thing that grows in the soil and makes its own food in the sun.
What living things eat and drink to stay alive.
What living things breathe. We cannot see it, but it is all around us.
Being warm, not too cold. Living things need warmth to stay well.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in You do not need to be a scientist to teach this unit. You….
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: You do not need to be a scientist to teach this unit. You only need to be curious with your child. When you do not know an answer, that is wonderful - you can wonder together, and you can ask your AI tutor. The most important thing you can give a young child is your warm attention and plenty of time to look, touch, and talk. Praise their thinking, not just their answers.

questions.question: What does “Living / Alive” mean?
options: Something that is alive. It grows, eats, drinks, and breathes.
Something that is not alive and never was alive.
A living thing that can move about and eats to live.
A living thing that grows in the soil and makes its own food in the sun.
questions.answer: Something that is alive. It grows, eats, drinks, and breathes.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Living / Alive means Something that is alive. It grows, eats, drinks, and breathes..

questions.question: What does “Non-living” mean?
options: Something that is not alive and never was alive.
A living thing that can move about and eats to live.
A living thing that grows in the soil and makes its own food in the sun.
questions.answer: Something that is not alive and never was alive.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Non-living means Something that is not alive and never was alive..

questions.question: What does “Animal” mean?
options: A living thing that can move about and eats to live.
A living thing that grows in the soil and makes its own food in the sun.
What living things eat and drink to stay alive.
questions.answer: A living thing that can move about and eats to live.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Animal means A living thing that can move about and eats to live..

questions.question: What does “Plant” mean?
options: A living thing that grows in the soil and makes its own food in the sun.
What living things eat and drink to stay alive.
What living things breathe. We cannot see it, but it is all around us.
questions.answer: A living thing that grows in the soil and makes its own food in the sun.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Plant means A living thing that grows in the soil and makes its own food in the sun..

questions.question: What does “Grow” mean?
options: What living things eat and drink to stay alive.
What living things breathe. We cannot see it, but it is all around us.
Being warm, not too cold. Living things need warmth to stay well.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Grow means To get bigger over time..

questions.question: What does “Food and Water” mean?
options: What living things eat and drink to stay alive.
What living things breathe. We cannot see it, but it is all around us.
Being warm, not too cold. Living things need warmth to stay well.
Something that is alive. It grows, eats, drinks, and breathes.
questions.answer: What living things eat and drink to stay alive.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Food and Water means What living things eat and drink to stay alive..

questions.question: Which science word matches this meaning: What living things breathe. We cannot see it, but it is all around us.?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: Air means What living things breathe. We cannot see it, but it is all around us..

questions.hint: Use the Unit 1 Science Words reference.

selfAssessment: I can connect it to your child's own body. They already know they feel hungry, thirsty, and cold. Use these everyday feelings as the doorway into science. When your child says, "I am hungry," you can say, "Yes! You are a living thing, and living things need food."
